title: Game Theory Foundations
description: This course introduces **the science of strategic decision-making**. Students learn how rational individuals, organizations, and nations make decisions when the outcome depends on the actions of others.

title: Strategic Decision Making
description: This course studies **how rational agents make strategic decisions** when outcomes depend on uncertainty, risk, and the choices of others. It develops the analytical tools used in economics, AI, business, politics, and negotiation.

title: Simultaneous Games
description: This course studies **games in which all players choose their strategies at the same time**, without knowing the decisions of the other players. It introduces the core analytical tools of classical game theory.

title: Sequential Games
description: This course studies **games in which players make decisions over time**, observing previous actions before making their own choices. It introduces game trees, backward induction, and dynamic strategic reasoning.

title: Nash Equilibrium
description: This course studies **Nash Equilibrium**, the central concept of game theory. It explains how stable outcomes emerge when every player chooses the best strategy given the choices of everyone else.

title: Repeated Games
description: This course studies **games that are played multiple times**, showing how repeated interactions can lead to cooperation, trust, reputation, and long-term strategic behavior.

title: Cooperative Game Theory
description: This course studies **how groups of players cooperate to achieve shared goals and fairly distribute the resulting benefits or costs**. It introduces coalition formation, bargaining, fairness, and cooperative decision-making.

title: Auction Theory
description: This course studies **how auctions allocate goods and determine prices through strategic bidding**. It explores bidder behavior, auction design, and the economics behind modern marketplaces.

title: Voting & Social Choice
description: This course studies **how groups combine individual preferences into collective decisions**. It explores voting systems, fairness, strategic voting, and the mathematical limits of collective decision-making.

title: Evolutionary Game Theory
description: This course studies **how strategies evolve over time within populations**, rather than assuming perfectly rational players. It explains how cooperation, competition, and adaptation emerge in biology, economics, sociology, and artificial intelligence.

title: Mechanism Design
description: This course studies **how to design rules, incentives, and institutions that produce desirable outcomes when individuals act in their own self-interest**. Unlike game theory, which analyzes existing games, mechanism design **creates the game**.

title: Applications of Game Theory
description: This capstone course studies **how game theory is applied across economics, business, politics, biology, computer science, artificial intelligence, and everyday decision-making**. It integrates the concepts from the previous courses through real-world systems and case studies.
